hints.junior: The 'works in dev, breaks in prod' pattern is almost always a hydration mismatch. The first thing to check: any Math.random(), Date.now(), or window reads in render.
hints.mid: Hydration errors in production are silent: no console warnings, just a flash of incorrect UI. That's why CLS is a useful proxy metric.
hints.staff: React 18's hydrateRoot gives you error boundaries around hydration failures. Use them to capture mismatch telemetry before users see the flash.

hints.junior: See how the ID changes every time you 're-render' in broken mode? That's the server ID and client ID being different. Fixed mode always shows the same stable value.
hints.mid: useId generates :rN: style IDs: the counter increments based on component tree position, which is identical on server and client.
hints.staff: useId is tied to the tree structure, not execution order. Conditional rendering can change the count; test useId in conditional branches.

hints.junior: If you see 'Text content did not match' in the console, that's React telling you exactly which component has the mismatch.
hints.mid: Grep for Math.random and new Date in your render functions: those are the two most common culprits. Move them to useEffect or useMemo with stable deps.
hints.staff: Selective hydration in React 18 means not every mismatch blocks interactivity, but it's still worth eliminating them for CLS health.

hints.junior: The concept chapter is the principle: read it before the demo so the toggle makes sense.
hints.mid: Connect the principle to your component API: what prop or CSS change encodes the fix?
hints.staff: Map the pattern to observability: what would you measure in RUM or lab to prove the fix?

anecdotes.junior: The first hydration mismatch I saw: a 'Last seen' timestamp that differed by one second between server and client. Moved it to useEffect; fixed immediately.
anecdotes.mid: I've audited apps where every form had random IDs: dozens of hydration warnings on every page load, silently fixed by client re-render, causing measurable CLS.
anecdotes.staff: The FOUC script pattern for themes is underappreciated: it's the cleanest way to read localStorage before React hydrates without any mismatch at all.

sections.junior: Hey! Today we're figuring out why components work fine in development but flicker or show wrong stuff in production. It's about hydration. Don't worry, we'll make it simple.
sections.mid: This case covers React hydration mismatches: what causes them, why they're silent in production, and three clean patterns to prevent them.
sections.staff: We're covering the server/client tree matching constraint, React 18's selective hydration model, and CLS as a production signal for hydration regressions.

sections.junior: The concept chapter is the principle: read it before the demo so the toggle makes sense.
sections.mid: Connect the principle to your component API: what prop or CSS change encodes the fix?
sections.staff: Map the pattern to observability: what would you measure in RUM or lab to prove the fix?

sections.junior: See how the ID changes every time you 're-render' in broken mode? That's the server ID and client ID being different. Fixed mode always shows the same stable value.
sections.mid: useId generates :rN: style IDs: the counter increments based on component tree position, which is identical on server and client.
sections.staff: useId is tied to the tree structure, not execution order. Conditional rendering can change the count; test useId in conditional branches.
